projects.title: Work Report to Portfolio Pipeline
projects.summary: Comprehensive design update documenting the full Work Report to Portfolio Site pipeline.

blocks.body: One skill I have struggled with is documentation. It takes work to record what I have done and organize it. My focus this semester is on refinement, so the past few weeks I have been building a pipeline to address exactly this. The goal is making recording what I do and displaying it as efficient as possible. Enter my first artifact; The Work Report to Portfolio Site pipeline… diagram! My naming prowess aside, this diagram shows the steps involved and how work I do gets recorded and transformed though the process. This update will go through each step of the pipeline, what it does, and why it's valuable.
## Artifact 1: Work Report to Portfolio Site Pipeline

blocks.alt: Work Report to Portfolio Site Pipeline
blocks.caption: Work Report to Portfolio Site Pipeline

blocks.body:
## Step 0: Work

The first step is doing stuff. Working on the pipeline has involved developing many programs to process and consolidate data, so I have created lots of lines of code. I also set up a 3D printer, so using that I ended up with a lot of pictures I take on my phone. Each new thing that I do has a different output, but putting everything together and keeping track of it all is a task on its own, and not one I particularly enjoy. That brings me to the goal.

**Pipeline's goal:** Record all work that I do, and make it as easy as possible to display it.

## Step 1: Capture

The first thing to do is to discover everything I do. I need to take the different outputs of work evidence and put them into a form that the pipeline can use. That form is registering the item and its location into a database. That gives a streamlined way to interact with all pieces of work evidence. This is different for every item, so I will just go through a few examples.

### Code

This is one I have been dealing with a lot recently, and the process is straight forward. Changes to code are often already tracked though version control. Every time a set of changes is made, you can commit those changes to the repository. Then you have a dedicated history of everything that has been done. When a commit is mode, the set of changes or the “diff” is logged to the database.

### Phone Pictures

Pictures on my phone need to be on my computer so the pipeline can access them. I am using “Syncthing”, a program that keeps my phone pictures and a local folder on my computer synchronized. Then I have a program that watches that folder for new items, adding each to the database as it gets downloaded.

### Graphs

Not every artifact can be automatically created, some need manual work to create them. The graph is an example of an artifact that never gets auto created, but I made a program that takes graph information and displays it. So when you use the program to create a graph, it gets added to the database.

**Full list of current imports:**

- screenshot-tool: screenshot_fullscreen, screenshot_region, screenshot_window (Produces: screenshot, image)
- diagram-tool: diagram_save (Produces: diagram - JSON, PNG, SVG)
- track-file: manual_registration (Produces: screenshot, diagram, data, comparison, gallery, video, document)
- chrome-automation: chrome_screenshot (Produces: screenshot)
- chrome_mcp: screenshot (Produces: screenshot)
- mermaid-tool: mermaid_from_template, mermaid_generate (Produces: mermaid)
- system-visualizer: system_flowchart, system_timeline, system_dependencies (Produces: mermaid)
- portfolio-editor: artifact_creation (Produces: image, video, doc, data, code, file)
- audio-tools: text_to_audio (Produces: audio)
- phone-camera-watcher: media_sync (Produces: phone-screenshot)
- conduit: image_generation (Produces: generated_image)
- git: commit diffs (Produces: diff)

## Reflection 1: How did you ensure traceability between requirements, constraints, and final design?

In software, constraints and requirements are often straightforward. Either it works, or it doesn’t. I have program x, it outputs y, your program needs to be able to use y. In this project, that was not the case. As you can see in this list, I deal with a massive amount of different formats, so the challenge has been trying to get a system that can handle all of them. But doing that requires special handling for each, that means In order to change a part of the system, it often requires many individual changes to support a structural one, even with the high amounts of modularity and streamlining I was adding. This caused a lot of soft constraints, few things were actually locked in, since I made all parts of the pipeline, but as more got added, it became harder and harder to change. I combatted this someone by making everything independent, so a change in step one wouldn’t require changes everywhere else. But the complexity of the pipeline made those strategies less effective.

## Step 2: Aggregate

All the items are now discoverable, but not organized. So the next step is taking all the pieces, wrapping them up, and adding context. Enter work reports!

These are nice to have, they group items together, allow for context searching, and can remind me what I was doing and why I made a change. But adding new forms of documentation doesn’t help my documentation issues. Fortunately, these are for internal use, so extra tools become available. In this case AI, specifically I have been using Z.ai’s GLM 5 running though the claude code CLI, but this setup works with any model capable of reasoning and visual understanding. With a program that feeds the model instructions on how to create work reports, all it takes is a few sentences and a work report is created automatically.

When the work report tool is used, all the items that have been registered to the database get gathered, and the model can go through them, see what changed or what was created, and make a work report with all the given context. All the items are then attached to the report, and registered as “artifacts”, I'll use the term "pipeline artifacts” to avoid confusion. Each pipeline artifact is a file attached to a report, allowing all the work evidence and context to be stored together.

**Full list of data types currently tracked:**

id, date, title, summary, description, reasoning, tags[], status, commit, files_changed, lines_added, lines_removed, diff_stat, duration_hours, effort, artifacts[]

## Step 3: Curate

Now that the data is packaged up, it’s more useful internally, but I am still no closer to having something in a presentable form. This step takes work reports, and all the data they have attached, and gives me an interface to put it all together. Behold, artifact number 2! My portfolio editor is a custom web building interface that unpacks work reports and allows building and editing presentable web pages.
## Artifact 2: Portfolio Editor
There are 3 editor tabs:

**Reports:**
Allows jumping into each work report and dragging and dropping each item into the main page.

**Projects:**
Keeps track of all the presentable pages and their data.

**Blocks:**
A bank of artifact types, without data, allows adding any kind of item to the page and filling in the information manually. This also keeps a list of blocks currently on a page.
And finally, it has save and deploy buttons.

## Step 4: Publish

So after the work evidence is collected, each type gets added to a database. An AI reads from the database with a short explanation to make high quality work reports with minimal effort. The editor is a tool to easily take the information from reports and build presentable display pages. And finally, when deploy is clicked in the editor, the changes get uploaded to my portfolio site. Along with that, Artifact 3, This project’s portfolio site detail page.
## Artifact 3: Work Report Pipeline Detail Page

I’m just kidding, you can use the link. I just think QR codes are cool.

https://nickyoungci.dev/projects/detail?project=work-report-to-portfolio-site-pipeline

projects.title: Artifact Pipeline: Config-Based Storage
projects.summary: Migrated artifact storage from dedicated database rows to JSONB config fields, simplifying the pipeline and aligning all three registries.

blocks.body: Moved artifact storage from its own database table into the existing operations table as JSONB config fields. This simplified the pipeline since artifacts are now just metadata attached to the operations that created them. Also unified the three separate registries (handlers, artifact sources, and type mappings) so they all agree on what types exist and how to handle them.

projects.title: Portfolio Site: Auto Icon Assignment
projects.summary: Automatic icon assignment for portfolio projects based on their tags, with a priority system for domain, skill, and fallback icons.

blocks.body: Built a service that automatically picks an icon for new portfolio projects based on their tags. Domain-specific tags like Wayland, ComfyUI, or Audio get priority, then skill tags like Python or React, with a generic code icon as the fallback. Also ran a backfill across all existing projects so none are left without an icon.

projects.title: Desktop HUD: GTK4 Layer Shell Overlay
projects.summary: Configurable desktop overlay using GTK4 and layer-shell, supporting images, videos, and live data widgets with a runtime HTTP API.
projects.previewAlt: Desktop HUD: GTK4 Layer Shell Overlay

blocks.body:
## Description:

Configurable desktop overlay using GTK4 and layer-shell, supporting images, videos, and graph-lib widgets on the desktop surface.

Key components:
- Element system: pluggable image, video, and graph elements with a base class pattern
- Runtime HTTP API (port 7820): CRUD for elements, config reload, edit mode toggle, profile management, /health endpoint
- Visual editor: drag-to-position overlay editing with snap-to-grid
- Profile/layout system: save, switch, and restore element arrangements
- Discovery: auto-discovers graph-lib providers for live data widgets
- Systemd service: runs as a user service

23 source files, registered in Systems with vcsh repo, api/gui/configurable/systemd-service traits.

## Reasoning:

Desktop-hud is a standalone application combining graph-lib visualizations, layer-shell compositing, and a runtime control API. Previously untracked in draft state, now fully registered.

## GTK4 Layer-Shell Overlay with Runtime API (Report #118, 2026-02-22)

Configurable desktop overlay using GTK4 and layer-shell, supporting images, videos, and graph-lib widgets on the desktop surface.

Key components:
- **Element system**: pluggable image, video, and graph elements with a base class pattern
- **Runtime HTTP API** (port 7820): CRUD for elements, config reload, edit mode toggle, profile management
- **Visual editor**: drag-to-position overlay editing with snap-to-grid
- **Profile/layout system**: save, switch, and restore element arrangements
- **Discovery**: auto-discovers graph-lib providers for live data widgets
- **Systemd service**: runs as a user service

23 source files, registered in Systems with vcsh repo, api/gui/configurable/systemd-service traits.

*28 files changed, +482 -5 lines*

projects.title: Desktop HUD: Layout-Driven Architecture
projects.summary: Made layout files the single source of truth for Desktop HUD element definitions, replacing the old dual-source config approach.

blocks.body: Reworked how Desktop HUD manages its display elements. Previously, element definitions lived in both the config file and layout files, creating a confusing dual-source situation. Now layout files are the single source of truth — they store the full element config including type, renderer, data provider, position, and size. The main config just declares which layouts to load at startup. Also added profile-based startup, CLI flags for profile selection, and a REST endpoint for additive profile loading.

projects.title: Mermaid Diagram Tooling and System Visualizer
projects.summary: Mermaid diagram generation pipeline with templates, AI-assisted creation, and auto-generated system visualizations.
projects.previewAlt: Mermaid Diagram Tooling and System Visualizer

blocks.body: Added Mermaid diagram support across three layers. The portfolio site gained a Mermaid block renderer so diagrams display natively on project pages. A new system-visualizer package auto-generates flowcharts, timelines, and dependency graphs from the systems history database. And a mermaid-tool package provides 9 templates, AI-assisted generation via Ollama, a CLI wizard, and syntax validation. All three tools register their output as discoverable artifacts for work reports.

projects.title: Portfolio Editor: Artifact Import Fixes
projects.summary: Improved artifact import mapping in the portfolio editor with shape-aware conversion and a migration tool for legacy data.

blocks.body: Made the portfolio editor smarter about how it converts work report artifacts into content blocks. Instead of a simple type-to-type mapping, the importer now inspects the shape of the data to pick the right block type — for example, inferring whether a data artifact should become a gallery or a comparison. Also built a one-time migration script to fix blocks that were imported with the old logic, with backup support for safe rollback.

projects.title: Wayfire Toplevel Capture Hardening
projects.summary: Reliability improvements to the Wayfire window capture protocol plugin — watchdog timers, output migration, and view lifecycle tracking.

blocks.body: Hardened the ext-toplevel-image-capture protocol plugin to handle real-world failure modes that come up during extended capture sessions. Added a watchdog timer that detects and recovers from stalled frame captures, output migration handling so capture survives monitor hotplug and workspace moves, and view lifecycle tracking to maintain capture when windows are created or destroyed. These are the kinds of edge cases that silently break recording pipelines in multi-monitor setups.

projects.title: Wayfire Layer Plugin
projects.summary: Wayfire compositor plugin that pins windows to the topmost scene layer with priority-based stacking and IPC control.

blocks.body: A Wayfire compositor plugin that keeps specified windows permanently above all others by pinning them to the root of the scene graph. Windows are matched by app_id namespace from config or pinned at runtime via IPC. A priority system controls stacking order among pinned windows, and original scene positions are preserved for clean restoration on unpin or shutdown.

projects.title: Whisker Menu: Wayland Protocol Support
projects.summary: Added Wayland focus tracking to XFCE''s Whisker Menu so it properly auto-hides when clicking away on Wayfire.

blocks.body: XFCE's Whisker Menu didn't know how to detect focus loss on Wayland, so clicking away from the menu on the Wayfire compositor wouldn't close it. Added Wayland toplevel protocol support so the menu can monitor window activation changes and auto-hide when something else gets focus — matching the behavior users expect from the X11 version. This required integrating a Wayland protocol into the existing GTK/XFCE plugin architecture in C++.

projects.title: Work Reports: Full Edit API
projects.summary: Added full edit support for work reports across CLI and MCP, making all report fields mutable after creation.

blocks.body: Expanded the work report editing API so that all report fields — including commit references, git stats, dates, and artifacts — can be modified after initial creation. Previously, correcting metadata required manual database edits. Now the same edit capabilities are available through the CLI, the MCP tool interface, and the core library, with proper validation and protection against accidentally modifying archived reports.

projects.title: Daily Check-In App
projects.summary: Expo React Native mobile app for daily self-tracking with subjective metrics, swipe navigation, and Syncthing-based sync.

blocks.body:
Built a complete Expo React Native mobile app for daily self-tracking from scratch. The app runs on Android via Expo Go or as a standalone APK and tracks subjective daily metrics over time.

## What the app does

Daily Check-In presents a single-screen daily entry form where users rate three metrics on a 1-5 scale (sleep quality, alertness, mood), optionally record dreams and notes as free text, and can manually override sleep/wake times using a draggable range slider. The app creates one entry per day, navigable by swiping left/right between days.

All data is stored as a single human-readable JSON file, designed for syncing via Syncthing across devices. The storage layer uses Android's Storage Access Framework (SAF) to let users pick any folder as the save location, with AsyncStorage as an automatic backup that self-heals if SAF writes fail.

## Architecture

**32 files, ~2100 lines of source code** (excluding package-lock.json and binary assets).

### App layer (expo-router)
- `app/_layout.tsx` — Root layout with gesture handler, status bar, notification response listener, cache pre-warming on startup
- `app/index.tsx` — Redirect to `/entry`
- `app/entry/index.tsx` — Main entry screen: rating rows, text inputs, sleep time toggle/slider, swipe gestures via react-native-gesture-handler fling detection, keyboard-aware layout
- `app/settings.tsx` — Settings screen: SAF folder picker, notification toggle with permission flow, time picker (scrollable hour bubbles + minute selector)

### Component layer
- `DateNav` — Day navigation header with prev/next arrows, long-press to open settings
- `RatingRow` — Reusable 1-5 bubble selector with selected state styling
- `SleepTimeBar` — Dual-handle range slider built on react-native-reanimated shared values and gesture-handler pan gestures. Maps a 22:00-14:00 (16-hour) range to slider positions with 15-minute snap grid. Labels update in real-time via `useAnimatedReaction` bridging the UI thread to JS state.

### Data layer
- `store.ts` — Core storage engine: SAF read/write via `StorageAccessFramework`, AsyncStorage backup, in-memory cache for synchronous day navigation, folder picker flow, safe write helper that handles SAF's delete-and-recreate pattern and deduplicates `daily-checkin (1).json` duplicates from Syncthing conflicts
- `types.ts` — TypeScript interfaces for Entry, DataFile, AppSettings
- `dateUtils.ts` — Date arithmetic using date-fns (format, navigate, day numbering from first entry)

### Hooks
- `useEntry` — Manages per-day entry state: loads from cache synchronously (falls back to async SAF read), tracks dirty state, auto-saves via 2-second debounce, provides `saveNow()` for navigation guards, handles initialization flag to prevent auto-saving freshly loaded data
- `useSettings` — Simple settings loader/saver over AsyncStorage

### Notifications
- `scheduler.ts` — Daily notification scheduling with Expo Notifications API. Gracefully handles Expo Go (where notifications aren't available) by dynamically requiring the module. Sets up Android notification channel, handles permission requests, schedules daily triggers.

### Sleep data provider
- `sleep_data_provider.py` — Python CLI that reads the JSON file and outputs structured sleep data for a given date. Handles truncated files (Syncthing partial writes) via `raw_decode`. Maps dream text to enum categories. Supports `DAILY_CHECKIN_JSON` env var override.

### Design
- Dark OLED theme (`#0f0f1a` background) with purple accent (`#6c5ce7`)
- Consistent design tokens: colors, spacing, radius, fontSize exported from `theme.ts`
- All UI built with React Native core components (no third-party UI library)

## Key technical decisions

- **SAF over expo-file-system paths**: Survives app reinstalls and works with any folder picker destination, including Syncthing sync folders
- **In-memory cache with async fallback**: Day navigation is instant (cache hit) while first load reads from SAF. Cache refreshes on app start.
- **Reanimated worklets for sleep slider**: Time-to-position math runs on the UI thread (`'worklet'` directive) for 60fps drag responsiveness, with `runOnJS` bridge for state updates
- **Dynamic require for notifications**: `expo-notifications` crashes in Expo Go, so it's loaded conditionally at runtime with a try/catch wrapper
- **Auto-save with initialization guard**: 2-second debounce saves as you type, but a 200ms delay after data load prevents the load itself from triggering a write-back

## Published to GitHub
https://github.com/Ckrest/daily-checkin-app — 32 tracked files, MIT license, clean standalone repo via vcsh with no Systems dependencies in published files.

This was a full greenfield app build — from `create-expo-app` scaffold to a working, published Android app with non-trivial features (animated range slider, SAF storage with conflict handling, notification scheduling, swipe navigation). High effort due to the breadth of concerns: gesture handling, animated UI, file system persistence with backup strategy, notification permissions, and the final publication pass. The sleep time slider alone required worklet-based position math, snap-to-grid logic, and animated reaction bridging.

projects.title: Portfolio Editor: Blueprint Architecture
projects.summary: Refactored the portfolio editor from a monolithic Flask app into a modular blueprint architecture with proper separation of concerns.

blocks.body: Broke apart the portfolio editor's monolithic Flask app into a clean blueprint architecture with separate modules for routes and business logic. Along the way, fixed a UX issue where the editor wasn't tracking unsaved changes properly — edits could be lost without warning. Added an "unsaved changes" prompt, a preview refresh button, and a test suite to keep things solid going forward.

projects.title: Portfolio Editor: Universal Detail Pages
projects.summary: Made every portfolio project link to its own detail page and fixed a false warning when saving preview images.

blocks.body: Simplified the portfolio site so every project always gets its own detail page instead of some linking directly to GitHub or external URLs. This removed a bunch of conditional logic and made the editor more consistent — you can always edit blocks for any project now. Also fixed a bug where dragging an artifact image onto the project header would show a false "preview not found" warning on save because the validation ran before the file was actually copied into place.

projects.title: Work Reports: Operations Architecture
projects.summary: Simplified the work report flow from a 7-step session process to a 2-step create-and-curate model with operations-based artifact discovery.

blocks.body: Major simplification of the work report creation flow. The old approach required 7+ MCP round-trips with complex session state tracked in the database. Replaced it with a straightforward create-then-curate model. Also reworked artifact discovery to query tool operations instead of a dedicated artifacts table, which fixed a data pollution issue and made it trivial to add new artifact sources. Removed about 700 lines of dead code from the old staging and curation systems.

projects.title: Camera Watcher: Phone Screenshot Support
projects.summary: Added phone screenshot support to the camera watcher so phone captures flow into work reports alongside desktop screenshots.

blocks.body: Extended the camera watcher service to support phone screenshots alongside desktop screenshots. Phone screenshots synced via Syncthing are now automatically detected, registered in the database, and available as artifacts during work report creation — the same end-to-end pipeline that already worked for desktop captures.

projects.title: Daily Check-In: UX Polish
projects.summary: Major UX polish pass on the Daily Check-In app — swipe navigation, auto-save, snappier controls, and keyboard handling improvements.

blocks.body: Focused UX polish session for the Daily Check-In Expo app. Added swipe navigation between days, auto-save so there's no manual save button, and cleaner settings access via long-press. Fixed keyboard handling quirks on Android and tightened up the layout so everything fits on one screen without scrolling. Pushed around 10 over-the-air updates to dial in the spacing on-device.

projects.title: Work Reports: Auto-Sweep Artifacts
projects.summary: Added automatic artifact discovery to the work report completion flow so agents don't need to manually track artifact IDs.

blocks.body: When an AI agent creates artifacts during a work report session, they used to need to manually pass artifact IDs back through the flow. This update makes the completion step automatically discover any artifacts registered during the session window. Also added a CLI flag for attaching artifacts to existing reports after the fact, and fixed a bug where the MCP command template left orphaned flags when optional parameters were empty.

projects.title: Sleep Tracker: 7-Day Dashboard
projects.summary: Sleep tracking dashboard with 7-day trends, multi-provider data pipeline, and settings-hub integration.

blocks.body:
## Description:

Major refactor of sleep-tracker data pipeline and settings-hub integration. Deleted settings_hub_bridge.py (418 lines) in favor of a new views.py module with build_day_view(target_date) and build_week_trends(). Added 'view day [--date]' and 'view week' CLI subcommands replacing the bridge's custom dispatch. Added provider refresh mechanism: collect_command/collect_timeout fields in provider config, refresh_all() with ThreadPoolExecutor parallelism, --refresh CLI flag and API query param. Fixed datetime.now() to use timezone.utc across providers and routes. Normalized 28K naive DB timestamps. Built 7-day tabbed settings-hub view with per-day sources using shell date arithmetic, overnight temp/humidity graphs, and tab_label field for dynamic date labels. Reversed tab order (oldest left, today right) with default_tab selecting today.

## Reasoning:

The settings_hub_bridge was a monolithic module coupling data access with presentation. The refactor separates concerns: views.py handles presentation, the CLI provides a clean interface for both humans and settings-hub sources, and the provider refresh mechanism enables upstream data pulls on demand rather than relying on separate cron-like scheduling.

projects.title: Work Reports: Agent Artifact Creation
projects.summary: Enabled AI agents to capture and register artifacts on the fly during the work report creation flow.

blocks.body: Previously, AI agents could only attach pre-existing artifacts to work reports. This update lets them actively create evidence during the report flow — taking browser screenshots, generating diagrams, or registering data files as they answer verification questions. The work report wizard now hints at which creation tools are available for each question, and a new generic registration tool handles artifact types that don't have dedicated capture tools.

projects.title: Systems Registry: Excludes Generation Fix
projects.summary: Fixed a silent git config bug that broke excludes files across all vcsh repos, and added auto-generation of per-package ignore patterns.

blocks.body: Discovered that git exclude files were silently non-functional across all vcsh repos due to a relative path issue — git couldn't resolve the paths because vcsh stores its git directories in a non-standard location. Fixed all repos to use absolute paths and built auto-generation into the sync tooling so each package gets its own excludes file with common ignore patterns. The result was going from thousands of noisy untracked files to clean status output across the board.

projects.title: Systems Registry: Full Health Audit
projects.summary: Full health audit of the Systems registry — fixed vcsh infrastructure, tracked orphaned integration files, and cleaned up packages across 14 repos.

blocks.body: Ran a comprehensive health audit across the Systems registry, touching 14 version-controlled repos in a single session. Fixed path handling bugs in the vcsh tooling, tracked down orphaned integration files that weren't being version-controlled, cleaned up stale paths and missing ignore patterns across several packages, and updated documentation. Ended with all 63 packages passing validation with zero untracked files or ownership violations.

projects.title: Portfolio Editor: Worktree Migration
projects.summary: Cleaned up the portfolio editor''s file tracking, config resolution, and added report card IDs to the sidebar UI.

blocks.body: Follow-up cleanup for the portfolio editor. Migrated file tracking to use package-relative paths (matching the vcsh convention used by all other packages), simplified the config resolution to use the shared systems path module instead of a manual fallback chain, and added report ID numbers to the sidebar cards so it's easier to cross-reference reports. Also cleaned up a test configuration hack that was causing validation warnings.

projects.title: Python Packaging: Editable Install Migration
projects.summary: Migrated 22 Python CLI packages from legacy launcher scripts to modern pip editable installs with proper entry points.

blocks.body: Replaced legacy launcher scripts across 22 Python CLI packages with modern pip editable installs. The old approach used fragile path-manipulation hacks that broke easily; the new setup uses standard pyproject.toml entry points that pip manages directly. This was a multi-session effort that included deleting old shim scripts, updating systemd service files, fixing MCP tool binary references, and cleaning up broken symlinks. Discovered an interesting edge case where flat-layout packages with identical module names collide in editable installs — a few packages were left with the old approach for their MCP tools until they can be properly restructured.

projects.title: Claude Code: Backend Restructure
projects.summary: Restructured Claude Code configuration for consistent behavior across multiple AI backends and modernized the permission system.

blocks.body: Unified the Claude Code setup so it works consistently whether using the default Anthropic API, a Z.ai proxy, or a local Ollama backend. The old approach used separate config directories per backend which caused lost settings and double-login issues. Also replaced a sprawling custom permission system (hundreds of regex rules) with a cleaner hook-based approach using community-maintained safety hooks for dangerous command blocking and credential protection.

projects.title: Work Reports: Core Extraction and YAML Fix
projects.summary: Extracted shared business logic into a core module and fixed YAML artifact parsing bugs in the work report system.
projects.previewAlt: Work Reports: Core Extraction and YAML Fix

blocks.body: Pulled the shared business logic out of the work report CLI into a dedicated core module that both the CLI and the MCP/agent layer import from. This eliminated circular imports and code duplication between the two entry points. Also fixed a bug where YAML-formatted artifacts weren't being parsed correctly during the portfolio import step.

projects.title: Artifact Pipeline: Declarative Source Registry
projects.summary: Replaced class-based evidence handlers with a declarative source registry for simpler artifact discovery configuration.

blocks.body: Replaced the class-based evidence handler system with a declarative artifact source registry. Instead of writing a handler class for each artifact type, sources are now defined as configuration entries with their discovery queries and type mappings. This made it much easier to add new artifact sources and removed a lot of boilerplate code.

projects.title: Scene Tracker
projects.summary: AI-powered scene tracking tool that uses local Ollama vision models to analyze and categorize visual content.

blocks.body: An AI-powered scene tracking tool that uses local Ollama vision models to analyze and categorize visual content. Designed with a workflow orchestration layer so analysis steps can be composed into pipelines, and a REST API for integration with other tools. Runs entirely locally with no cloud dependencies.

projects.title: Work Reports: Package Restructure
projects.summary: Reorganized the work report package layout and stabilized the database schema for long-term maintainability.

blocks.body: Major restructure of the work report package — reorganized the module layout, cleaned up imports, and stabilized the PostgreSQL schema with proper migrations. The goal was to get the codebase into a maintainable shape after rapid feature development had left things scattered across too many files with tangled dependencies.

projects.title: ComfyUI Viewer: Standalone Distribution
projects.summary: Hardened the ComfyUI Viewer into a standalone package that works independently of the Systems framework.

blocks.body: Refactored the ComfyUI Viewer — a real-time image viewer for ComfyUI generation outputs — into a properly distributable standalone package. Removed hard dependencies on the Systems framework, cleaned up the architecture, and made it installable as an independent tool while still integrating cleanly when running inside the full environment.

projects.title: Systems Registry: Dynamic Trait API
projects.summary: Redesigned the trait system so traits define their own schema and API behavior, replacing hardcoded transforms with declarative definitions.

blocks.body: Major architecture change to how the Systems registry handles traits. Previously, trait data was embedded in package manifests and the API needed hardcoded transforms to serve it. The new design moves trait data into separate files and lets each trait define its own schema, resolution logic, and API output format. Packages just provide data — the API serves it dynamically without code changes. Also overhauled the trait vocabulary, adding new traits and retiring ones that no longer fit the model.

projects.title: Portfolio Editor Update
projects.summary: Architecture overhaul and feature additions for the portfolio editor — modular ES modules, observer pattern, connection pooling, archive/import workflow, and click-to-edit overlays.

blocks.body:
## Description:

Web-based split-pane GUI for building portfolio project pages from work reports. Flask backend (port 9750) with vanilla JS frontend. Features: report browser with archive/unarchive, project CRUD with settings/content separation, block-based content editor, drag-drop from reports, live preview with click-to-edit overlays, tag autocomplete from controlled vocabulary, import report as project, deploy staging/production workflow.

## Reasoning:

Built to replace manual YAML editing for portfolio project pages. The tool integrates work reports (stored in PostgreSQL) with portfolio projects (stored as YAML files). v2 architecture replaced legacy 6-file JS with modular ES modules using observer pattern to break circular dependencies. Event delegation eliminates memory leaks. Connection pooling prevents database exhaustion. Archive feature keeps reports panel uncluttered. Import feature provides one-click project creation from reports. I like cats

projects.title: Work Reports: Interactive Creation Wizard
projects.summary: Redesigned work report creation as an interactive wizard that guides through evidence review and verification steps.

blocks.body: Redesigned work report creation from a single-shot CLI command into an interactive wizard. The new flow walks through four phases: entering required fields, reviewing auto-discovered evidence (git diffs, screenshots, etc.), answering verification questions about what was done and why, and confirming the final report. The old approach tried to infer work duration from file timestamps, which was unreliable — now duration is always stated explicitly.

projects.title: Work Reports: Version Control Integration
projects.summary: Added vcsh support to work reports so git diffs and commit tracking work correctly in the multi-repo Systems environment.

blocks.body: Added vcsh multi-repo support to the work report system so git diffs and commit tracking work correctly in the Systems environment. Previously, git operations assumed a single repo per directory — but Systems uses vcsh to overlay multiple repos in the same home directory. The fix detects when a project corresponds to a vcsh repo and routes all git commands through vcsh accordingly.

projects.title: Artifact Pipeline: Curation Workflow
projects.summary: Built a git-inspired staging area where work report artifacts are reviewed, AI-scored for relevance, and curated before final attachment.

blocks.body: Added a staging workflow for work report artifacts, modeled after git's staging area concept. Screenshots, code snippets, and other evidence from a work session get collected into a pool where they can be reviewed and curated before being attached to the final report. Includes AI-powered relevance scoring using local vision models to help sort useful artifacts from noise, while keeping human oversight for the final call.

projects.title: Artifact Pipeline: Hook System Integration
projects.summary: Added hook-based artifact integration to the diagram and screenshot tools so captures are automatically tracked in the database.

blocks.body: Wired up the diagram tool and screenshot tool to automatically publish events when they create files, so those artifacts get tracked in the database and show up during work report creation. The integration uses hooks rather than direct dependencies — the tools themselves stay standalone with zero Systems imports, and the hook adapters handle the event publishing. This keeps the tools distributable as independent packages while still getting rich integration when running in the Systems environment.

projects.title: Artifact Pipeline: Typed Evidence System
projects.summary: Built the full artifacts pipeline connecting work reports to the portfolio site, with typed evidence blocks and copy-at-capture file storage.

blocks.body: Built the pipeline that turns daily work reports into portfolio content. Added new block types for code, terminal output, comparisons, and graphs to the portfolio site, then wired up the work report system to capture artifacts (screenshots, diffs, code snippets) and store them durably at creation time. The portfolio editor can then pull these in with drag-and-drop curation, automatically creating the right block type for each artifact. The goal was to make the daily reporting workflow do double duty as content creation for the portfolio.

projects.title: Work Reports: Copy-at-Capture Artifacts
projects.summary: Switched artifact storage from file references to copy-at-capture, ensuring work report files are durable regardless of source location.

blocks.body: Changed how work report artifacts are stored — instead of just saving a reference to the original file location, artifacts are now copied into dedicated per-report folders at the moment they're captured. This means report files survive even if the original source gets moved or deleted, and each report is self-contained with all its evidence.

projects.title: Portfolio Site: Artifact Display Fix
projects.summary: Fixed artifact display across the portfolio editor and live site, including video blocks, path handling, and deploy cache-busting.

blocks.body: Fixed artifacts not displaying correctly across the portfolio site and editor. Images dragged from work reports into the editor were showing as broken because the path resolver didn't account for absolute filesystem paths in the editor context. Also got video blocks working properly with both local files and YouTube/Vimeo embeds, added preview cards for non-image artifact types, and set up cache-busting headers on Cloudflare Pages so deploys are immediately visible.

projects.title: Portfolio Site: Build Validation
projects.summary: Added sensible defaults and build-time validation to portfolio detail pages so every project renders cleanly.

blocks.body: Improved the portfolio detail pages so every project has a consistent, well-structured header section and guaranteed content. Projects without explicit content blocks now automatically fall back to displaying their README. The build process also gained validation warnings that catch broken links, placeholder summaries, and empty blocks before they make it to the live site.

projects.title: Portfolio Editor: Block Group UX
projects.summary: Added group block editing UX to the portfolio editor with add buttons, click-to-deselect, and drag-leave cleanup.

blocks.body: Improved the group block editing experience in the portfolio editor. Groups now have a permanent "add" button for inserting sub-blocks, clicking empty space deselects the current block, and dragging a block type out of the preview area properly cleans up visual indicators. Also fixed click event routing so the editor's overlay UI doesn't interfere with the block controls underneath.

projects.title: Portfolio Site: Block-Based Content
projects.summary: Replaced the rigid detail page renderer with a composable block system for flexible, editor-friendly project pages.

blocks.body: Replaced the portfolio site's rigid detail page system (which had separate render modes for readme, pdf, structured, and minimal pages) with a flexible block-based content model. Each project page is now composed from an ordered array of typed blocks — text, images, videos, galleries, code, and more — that can be freely arranged in the editor. This also simplified the project schema and added a live preview API so the editor can show changes in real time.

projects.title: Portfolio Site: Modular Architecture
projects.summary: Rebuilt the portfolio site from a single monolithic HTML file into a modular component architecture with structured project data.

blocks.body: Rebuilt the portfolio site from scratch, replacing a 900-line monolithic HTML file with a modular architecture. Each section (header, hero, timeline, etc.) now lives in its own module, CSS is organized into a token-based design system, and every project has its own settings.yaml that gets built into a manifest at deploy time. The main motivation was making project data machine-readable so external tools (like the portfolio editor) could create and update projects programmatically rather than through manual YAML editing.

projects.title: Portfolio Editor
projects.summary: Web-based portfolio editor with work report integration, drag-and-drop blocks, and live preview.

blocks.body: Built the first version of a web-based editor for managing portfolio projects. The key idea is that it connects directly to the work report database, so instead of manually writing project descriptions, you can drag data from completed work reports into new portfolio entries. The editor has a split-pane layout with project settings, a block-based content editor with drag-and-drop, and a live preview that shows exactly how the portfolio page will look. Supports multiple block types including text, images, video, galleries, and code.

projects.title: Modular Flow Engine: Unified Outputs
projects.summary: Simplified the flow engine''s output system by replacing complex persistence logic with a clean destinations model.

blocks.body: Replaced the flow engine's complex resume/persistence system with a simpler output destinations model. Instead of managing state for paused and resumed flows, sinks now just declare where their output goes — return it as an API response, write it to a file, or print it to console. This made the codebase significantly simpler and the mental model much clearer for anyone building flows.

projects.title: ComfyUI Conduit: Redis Event Fix
projects.summary: Fixed a broken import path that was preventing ComfyUI Conduit from publishing Redis events for the real-time gallery.

blocks.body: The real-time gallery updates for ComfyUI-generated images had stopped working because the event publishing module couldn't be found after a directory restructure. A quick one-line path fix got Redis events flowing again so the gallery picks up new images as they're generated. Also cleaned up the README and pushed the conduit package to GitHub.

projects.title: Docklike Wayland: Hover and Throttle Fix
projects.summary: Fixed hover glitches and improved window preview throttling in the Docklike taskbar plugin for Wayland.
projects.previewAlt: Docklike Wayland: Hover and Throttle Fix

blocks.body: Fixed visual glitches in the Docklike taskbar plugin where hover states were getting stuck, causing phantom menu popups and highlighting artifacts. Also reworked the window preview capture throttling so that all windows get live thumbnail updates instead of only the most recent one monopolizing the refresh queue. The result is a smoother, more responsive taskbar experience on Wayland.

projects.title: Docklike Wayland: Taskbar with Window Thumbnails
projects.summary: Ported docklike-plugin from X11 to Wayland with live window thumbnail captures and foreign-toplevel protocol integration.
projects.previewAlt: Docklike Wayland: Taskbar with Window Thumbnails

blocks.body:
## Description:

Ported docklike-plugin to Wayland with CaptureManager for async captures, WaylandWnck for window tracking

## Reasoning:

Original plugin was X11-only; Wayland required ext-foreign-toplevel and ext-image-capture-source protocols

projects.title: Wayfire Capture: Duplicate Title Fix
projects.summary: Fixed window capture in Wayfire so windows with identical titles get their own distinct thumbnails.

blocks.body: Fixed a bug in Wayfire's image capture protocol where windows with the same title (like multiple terminal instances) would all end up with the same thumbnail. The original code was matching windows by name, so duplicates always resolved to the first match. Switched to using direct object references for reliable per-window capture.

projects.title: Wlroots: Capture Buffer Format Fix
projects.summary: Fixed wlroots image capture by setting explicit buffer format constraints for GPU compatibility.

blocks.body: Patched wlroots to explicitly declare buffer format constraints for image capture sources. The default behavior wasn't providing the right format hints, which caused compatibility issues with certain GPU drivers. Setting explicit SHM and DMA-BUF formats with linear modifiers fixed the capture pipeline.

projects.title: Shutdown Effect
projects.summary: Visual shutdown animations with extensible hook system for power transitions on Wayland.

blocks.body: A Wayfire compositor plugin that plays visual animations during system power transitions — shutdown, reboot, and suspend. Ships with a fade animation as the default, and uses a hook-based discovery system so users can drop in custom animations (like this fire example) via XDG config directories without modifying the package itself. Works alongside a companion power-manager that handles the actual power state transitions.

projects.title: XFCE 4.20 Native Migration
projects.summary: Migrated XFCE desktop components from custom forks to native 4.20, rebasing patches and archiving unneeded forks.

blocks.body: Migrated three XFCE desktop components from custom forks to the native 4.20 release. Archived the clipman fork after confirming it had zero custom changes, rebased the panel fork to pick up 11 upstream commits while preserving the thin-strip and custom button-width patches, and kept the systemd fork that's still needed for Wayfire session integration. Each component was tested independently and the whole setup ran stable for 24+ hours before calling it done.

projects.title: Portfolio Site Update
projects.summary: Timeline improvements, media support for GIFs/videos, and bundle icon previews.

blocks.body: Batch of improvements to the portfolio site — updated the timeline layout, added support for GIFs and embedded videos in project detail pages, and added icon previews for project bundles on the main listing.

projects.title: Extension Dev Connector
projects.summary: HTTP bridge that lets AI agents reload, enable, and disable browser extensions during development via MCP tools.

blocks.body: An HTTP server that bridges browser extension management to MCP tools. During extension development, AI agents can use this connector to reload extensions after code changes, toggle them on/off for testing, and check their status — all without manual browser interaction.

projects.title: Monitor Detect
projects.summary: Wayland monitor detection script that identifies the primary display and exports it to the systemd environment.

blocks.body: Detects connected monitors on Wayland and exports the primary monitor information to the systemd user environment. Other services and scripts can then reference the correct display without hardcoding monitor names, which is especially useful when monitors change between docked and undocked setups.

projects.title: ComfyUI Conduit
projects.summary: Socket tagging system for ComfyUI that enables external workflow execution with typed inputs and captured outputs.

projects.title: Graph Library
projects.summary: Embeddable GTK4 graph widgets for time series visualization and gauges with a pluggable data provider architecture.

blocks.body: A library of embeddable GTK4 graph widgets designed for time series data and gauges. Uses a pluggable architecture that separates data sources (providers) from visualization (renderers), so the same data can be displayed as line charts, gauges, or other formats without changing the data layer.

projects.title: Ping Monitor
projects.summary: Lightweight network monitoring service that pings configured hosts and stores results in SQLite for historical analysis.

blocks.body: A simple network monitoring service that periodically pings a list of configured hosts and records the results in a SQLite database. Useful for tracking network reliability over time and spotting intermittent connectivity issues.

projects.title: Soundboard
projects.summary: GTK4 soundboard app with virtual microphone output via PipeWire, thumbnail previews, and tabbed organization.

blocks.body: A desktop soundboard built with GTK4 and libadwaita that routes audio through a PipeWire virtual microphone, so sounds play directly into calls and voice chat. Supports drag-and-drop import, thumbnail previews for each clip, and tabbed categories for organizing large sound libraries. Designed around PipeWire's virtual device system rather than loopback hacks, so it integrates cleanly with any app that uses the default mic input.

projects.title: ComfyUI Viewer
projects.summary: A web-based image viewer and workflow manager for ComfyUI with thumbnail generation and organization features.

projects.title: Portfolio Site
projects.summary: Personal portfolio website with project detail pages, timeline view, and a block-based content system.

projects.title: Agent Config Generator
projects.summary: Simple CLI that copies shared AI agent config files into the right places across projects.

blocks.body: A small utility that keeps AI agent configuration files in sync across projects. You define your shared conventions in one place, and the tool copies them out to wherever they need to go. Output paths are configurable so it works for anyone who clones it.

projects.title: Model Manager
projects.summary: Queue and job management system for concurrent Ollama inference across multiple evaluation systems.

blocks.body: A VRAM-aware job scheduler for Ollama that manages concurrent inference across multiple models. Tracks available GPU memory and queues requests so large models don't compete for VRAM, with automatic model loading and unloading. Provides both an HTTP API and CLI for submitting jobs, checking queue status, and managing model availability. Built to handle the workflow of running many different models throughout the day without manually juggling which ones are loaded.

projects.title: Systems Registry
projects.summary: Traits-based package registry managing ~60 packages with validation, vcsh version control, and MCP tool integration.

blocks.body: The core of the Systems infrastructure — a package registry that manages ~60 packages across four tiers using a composable traits system. Each package declares its capabilities (CLI, Python, systemd service, etc.) via traits, and the registry validates compliance, generates excludes files for vcsh version control, and exposes everything through MCP tools so AI agents can discover and work with packages. Evolved from a simple manifest schema into a full validation and publishing pipeline.

projects.title: Night Mode
projects.summary: Automated day/night display switching service that adjusts screen warmth based on sunrise and sunset times.

blocks.body: A background service that automatically switches display warmth between day and night modes based on local sunrise and sunset times. Uses an event-driven architecture with signal-based scheduling so it responds instantly to transitions with near-zero CPU usage. Includes a manual override toggle hook for when you want to force night or day mode.

projects.title: Wallpaper Service
projects.summary: Unified wallpaper daemon for Wayland with seamless hot-swap transitions between images.

blocks.body: A wallpaper daemon for Wayland that provides smooth transitions when swapping wallpapers. Runs as a background service and handles wallpaper changes seamlessly, including support for per-monitor wallpapers on multi-display setups.

projects.title: ComfyUI Easy Randomize
projects.summary: ComfyUI node pack for random selection with freeze and lock controls, designed for iterative prompt building.

blocks.body: A set of 10 ComfyUI nodes for adding controlled randomness to image generation workflows. The core idea is random selection with freeze and lock — you can randomize choices across text prompts, LoRAs, images, and numbers, then freeze a value you like while continuing to randomize everything else. This makes iterative prompt building much faster since you can lock in good elements one at a time instead of re-rolling everything. Includes dedicated nodes for different data types and a multi-choice text node for building prompt variations.

projects.title: ComfyUI Ollama Nodes
projects.summary: Custom ComfyUI nodes that integrate local Ollama LLMs for text generation and image analysis within workflows.

blocks.body: ComfyUI plugin that adds nodes for interacting with local Ollama language models. Lets you use LLMs for prompt generation, image captioning, and text processing directly inside ComfyUI workflows without needing cloud API keys.

projects.title: ComfyUI QuickSave
projects.summary: ComfyUI toolbar button for quickly saving preview images to disk with a single click.

blocks.body: Adds a one-click save button to the ComfyUI toolbar for quickly saving images from the preview pane. Handy when you want to keep a generated image without wiring up a full save workflow in the graph.

projects.title: Modular Flow Engine
projects.summary: A declarative dataflow engine for orchestrating multi-step AI evaluation pipelines with JSON-defined workflows, checkpointing, and error recovery.

projects.title: Vision Rename
projects.summary: CLI tool that batch-renames images using local Ollama vision models to generate descriptive filenames.

blocks.body: A command-line tool that uses local Ollama vision models to automatically rename image files with descriptive names. Send a folder of screenshots or photos and each image gets analyzed by the LLM, which generates a meaningful filename based on what it sees in the image.

projects.title: Virtual Camera Toggle
projects.summary: Simple bash script to toggle the OBS virtual camera on/off with a keyboard shortcut.

blocks.body: A minimal bash script that toggles the OBS virtual camera with a single keyboard shortcut. Useful for quickly enabling or disabling the virtual camera feed during video calls without switching to the OBS window.

projects.title: Wayfire Screen Freeze
projects.summary: Wayfire plugin that freezes the screen output for presentations, screenshots, or when you need a stable display.

blocks.body: A Wayfire compositor plugin that lets you freeze the screen output in place. Useful for taking stable screenshots, presenting a static view during screen sharing, or temporarily holding the display while making changes behind the scenes.

projects.title: RVM Virtual Camera
projects.summary: Real-time background removal for webcams using the Robust Video Matting neural network, streamed to a virtual camera device.

blocks.body: Uses the Robust Video Matting (RVM) neural network to do real-time background removal on webcam video, then streams the result to a virtual camera device. Other applications see a clean video feed with the background removed, no green screen needed.

projects.title: Desktop Layer
projects.summary: Lightweight clickable desktop surface for Wayfire and other wlroots-based Wayland compositors.

blocks.body: A desktop surface layer for wlroots-based Wayland compositors like Wayfire. Provides a clickable desktop background that can host widgets and respond to user interaction, something that Wayland compositors don't provide natively the way X11 window managers did.

projects.title: Settings Hub
projects.summary: GTK4 desktop dashboard with a tile-based layout system for monitoring and controlling system services.

blocks.body:
## Catalog Refactor & Provider Error-Surfacing (Report #68, 2026-02-12)

Settings Hub catalog refactor and provider error-surfacing test coverage. Major restructuring spanning 45 files with +5380 -2152 lines across Python refactoring, bug fixes, frontend improvements, and architectural changes.

*45 files changed, +5380 -2152 lines, ~180 hours effort*

## Graph-Lib Renderer Port to Tile Handlers (Report #103, 2026-02-20)

Ported both graph-lib renderers (LineChartRenderer, GaugeRenderer) into settings-hub tile handlers so all visualization features are YAML-declarable.

- **GraphTileHandler**: Full rewrite with axes, configurable grid, y/x-tick labels, current value pill, fill area, data points, rotated y-axis label, min/max annotations
- **GaugeTileHandler**: Configurable threshold colors, proportional arc width, value format control, optional label below value

Updated builtin tile configs and converted sleep-tracker's 5 sparkline tiles to full graph tiles with per-graph color themes.

## V4 Schema Upgrade & Ecosystem Integration (Report #105, 2026-02-20)

Major version upgrade from V2 to V4 tile set schema. Removed legacy backends (dconf/xfconf/composite) and entire module subsystem (~4,200 net lines deleted). Replaced config_file/service bindings with named sources (polled commands), actions, and hub_variables. Decomposed monolithic tile handler into content/display/input/layout modules. Added 14 new tile types (stack, tabs, table, sparkline, etc.).

Added settings-hub subcommands to night-mode, ping-monitor, and soundboard for machine-readable status payloads.

## Graph Gap Handling, Async Buttons, Dynamic Tab Labels (Report #111, 2026-02-21)

Three independent enhancements to the settings-hub tile framework:

1. **Graph gap handling**: Graph tiles handle None/gap values by breaking lines, fills, and points at gaps instead of drawing through them
2. **Async button actions**: Button tile actions run in background threads with visual feedback (disabled state + "Running..." label during execution, success/error feedback on completion)
3. **Dynamic tab labels**: Tabs support label_source/label_key properties for dynamic labels from source data

*7 files changed, +1972 -401 lines*

projects.title: Diagram Tool
projects.summary: A Figma-like diagramming tool for flowcharts and system diagrams. Single-user, local-first with JSON file storage.

projects.title: Screenshot Tool
projects.summary: Modular screenshot utility for Wayland with region, window, and fullscreen capture modes.

blocks.body:
## Modular Python Package Refactor (Report #17, 2026-01-21)

Consolidated screenshot-tool from a monolithic 1100-line script plus bash wrapper into a proper Python package with 12 focused modules. Fixed intermittent window capture failures caused by double-tap detection running before argument parsing. Added configurable paths via environment variables. Published to GitHub.

The original design had double-tap detection running before CLI argument parsing, which caused explicit commands like --window to be incorrectly intercepted as double-taps during rapid invocations. The modular structure improves maintainability and allows standalone distribution.

projects.title: PostgreSQL History Database
projects.summary: Central PostgreSQL database for tracking system operations, events, and tool activity across the Systems environment.

blocks.body: The shared PostgreSQL database that stores operation logs, event history, and tool activity for the Systems environment. Provides the data backbone for work reports, artifact tracking, and the various dashboards. Includes an event-driven db-logger that records operations as they happen via Redis pub/sub.

projects.title: Process Manager
projects.summary: GTK4 dashboard for managing Systems-discovered systemd user services with status, controls, and live logs.

blocks.body: A GTK4 desktop application for managing systemd user services discovered by the Systems registry. Shows service status at a glance, provides start/stop/restart controls, and streams live journal logs — all in a clean dashboard interface. Built to make it easy to manage the various background services that power the Systems environment.

projects.title: AI Integration Project Proposal
projects.summary: Project proposal outlining an independent AI integration engineering initiative.

blocks.body: A formal project proposal for an independent AI integration engineering initiative. Covers the strategic case for building internal AI tooling, with detailed milestones and a phased rollout plan. Written as a standalone document to present to stakeholders.
